!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allpaths.dump
documentation:
%prog dump fastbfile

Export ALLPATHS fastb file to fastq file. Use --dir to indicate a previously
run allpaths folder.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allpaths.fixpairs
documentation:
%prog fixpairs pairsfile sep sd

Fix pairs library stats. This is sometime useful to modify library stats,
for example, the separation between paired reads after importing the data.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allpaths.fill
documentation:
%prog fill frag_reads_corr.fastb

Run FillFragments on `frag_reads_corr.fastb`.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allpaths.extract_pairs
documentation:
Take fastqfile and array of pair ID, extract adjacent pairs to outfile.
Perform check on numbers when done. p1fw, p2fw is a list of file handles,
each for one end. p is a Pairs instance.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allpaths.pairs
documentation:
%prog pairs pairsfile <fastbfile|fastqfile>

Parse ALLPATHS pairs file, and write pairs IDs and single read IDs in
respective ids files: e.g. `lib1.pairs.fastq`, `lib2.pairs.fastq`,
and single `frags.fastq` (with single reads from lib1/2).

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allpaths.prepare
documentation:
%prog prepare "B. oleracea" *.fastq

Scan input fastq files (see below) and create `in_groups.csv` and
`in_libs.csv`. The species name does not really matter.

modules.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap
documentation:
Use genetic map to break chimeric scaffolds, order and orient scaffolds onto
chromosomes.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.blat
documentation:
%prog blat map1.txt ref.fasta

Make ALLMAPS input csv based on sequences. The tab-delimited txt file
include: name, LG, position, sequence.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.dotplot
documentation:
%prog dotplot map.csv ref.fasta

Make dotplot between chromosomes and linkage maps.
The input map is csv formatted, for example:

ScaffoldID,ScaffoldPosition,LinkageGroup,GeneticPosition
scaffold_2707,11508,1,0
scaffold_2707,11525,1,1.2

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.read_subsampled_matrix
documentation: Read the subsampled matrix from file if it exists, otherwise calculate it.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.draw_geneticmap_heatmap
documentation: Draw the heatmap of the genetic map.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.heatmap
documentation:
%prog heatmap map

Calculate pairwise linkage disequilibrium given MSTmap.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.header
documentation:
%prog header map conversion_table

Rename lines in the map header. The mapping of old names to new names are
stored in two-column `conversion_table`.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.rename
documentation:
%prog rename map markers.bed > renamed.map

Rename markers according to the new mapping locations.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.anchor

Anchor scaffolds based on map.

Convert MSTMAP output into bed format.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.geneticmap.fasta

Extract marker sequences based on map.

Find scaffold breakpoints using genetic map. Use variation.vcf.mstmap() to

documentation:
Print the genetic map in the BED format.

Args:
    filename (str, optional): Output filename. Defaults to "stdout".
    switch (bool, optional): Use linkage group as seqid. Defaults to False.
    sep (str, optional): Separator that delimits scaffold name and position. Defaults to ".".

modules.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess
documentation: Wrapper to trim and correct sequence data.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.diginorm
documentation:
%prog diginorm fastqfile

Run K-mer based normalization. Based on tutorial:
<http://ged.msu.edu/angus/diginorm-2012/tutorial.html>

Assume input is either an interleaved pairs file, or two separate files.

To set up khmer:
$ git clone git://github.com/ged-lab/screed.git
$ git clone git://github.com/ged-lab/khmer.git
$ cd screed
$ python setup.py install
$ cd ../khmer
$ make test
$ export PYTHONPATH=~/export/khmer

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.expand
documentation:
%prog expand bes.fasta reads.fastq

Expand sequences using short reads. Useful, for example for getting BAC-end
sequences. The template to use, in `bes.fasta` may just contain the junction
sequences, then align the reads to get the 'flanks' for such sequences.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.contamination
documentation:
%prog contamination Ecoli.fasta genome.fasta read.fastq

Check read contamination on a folder of paired reads. Use bowtie2 to compare
the reads against:
1. Ecoli.fsata - this will tell us the lower bound of contamination
2. genome.fasta - this will tell us the upper bound of contamination

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.count
documentation:
%prog count *.gz

Count reads based on FASTQC results. FASTQC needs to be run on all the input
data given before running this command.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.hetsmooth
documentation:
%prog hetsmooth reads_1.fq reads_2.fq jf-23_0

Wrapper against het-smooth. Below is the command used in het-smooth manual.

$ het-smooth --kmer-len=23 --bottom-threshold=38 --top-threshold=220
       --no-multibase-replacements --jellyfish-hash-file=23-mers.jf
           reads_1.fq reads_2.fq

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.trim
documentation:
%prog trim fastqfiles

Trim reads using TRIMMOMATIC. If two fastqfiles are given, then it invokes
the paired reads mode. See manual:

<http://www.usadellab.org/cms/index.php?page=trimmomatic>

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.preprocess.correct
documentation:
%prog correct *.fastq

Correct the fastqfile and generated corrected fastqfiles. This calls
assembly.allpaths.prepare() to generate input files for ALLPATHS-LG. The
naming convention for your fastqfiles are important, and are listed below.

By default, this will correct all PE reads, and remove duplicates of all MP
reads, and results will be placed in `frag_reads.corr.{pairs,frags}.fastq`
and `jump_reads.corr.{pairs,frags}.fastq`.

modules.name: jcvi.assembly.soap
documentation: Script to write and assist SOAPdenovo assembly.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.soap.correct

Correct reads using ErrorCorrection. Only PE will be used to build the K-mer
table.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.soap.clean

Model kmer distribution given error rate. See derivation in FIONA paper:

Compute log likelihood between two db.

Analyze K-mer histogram.

Returns:

modules.name: jcvi.assembly.hic
documentation: Process Hi-C output into AGP for chromosomal-scale scaffolding.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.hmean_int
documentation: Harmonic mean of an array, returns the closest int

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.golden_array
documentation:
Given list of ints, we aggregate similar values so that it becomes an
array of multiples of phi, where phi is the golden ratio.

phi ^ 14 = 843
phi ^ 33 = 7881196

So the array of counts go between 843 to 788196. One triva is that the
exponents of phi gets closer to integers as N grows. See interesting
discussion here:
<https://www.johndcook.com/blog/2017/03/22/golden-powers-are-nearly-integers/>

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.prune_tour_worker
documentation: Worker thread for CLMFile.prune_tour()

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.fit_power_law
documentation:
Fit power law distribution.

See reference:
http://mathworld.wolfram.com/LeastSquaresFittingPowerLaw.html
Assumes the form Y = A * X^B, returns

Args:
    xs ([int]): X vector
    ys ([float64]): Y vector

Returns:
    (A, B), the coefficients

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.dist
documentation:
%prog dist input.dist.npy genome.json

Plot histogram based on .dist.npy data file. The .npy file stores an array
with link counts per dist bin, with the bin starts stored in the genome.json.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.generate_groups
documentation:
Parse 'groups' file. The 'groups' file has the following format,
for example:

seq1,seq2 b
seq1 g
seq2 g

Args:
    groupsfile (str): Path to the groups file

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.read_matrix
documentation: Read the matrix from the npy file and apply log transformation and thresholding.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.draw_hic_heatmap
documentation:
Draw heatmap based on .npy file. The .npy file stores a square matrix with
bins of genome, and cells inside the matrix represent number of links
between bin i and bin j. The `genome.json` contains the offsets of each
contig/chr so that we know where to draw boundary lines, or extract per
contig/chromosome heatmap.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.heatmap
documentation:
%prog heatmap input.npy genome.json

Plot heatmap based on .npy data file. The .npy stores a square matrix with
bins of genome, and cells inside the matrix represent number of links
between bin i and bin j. The `genome.json` contains the offsets of each
contig/chr so that we know where to draw boundary lines, or extract per
contig/chromosome heatmap.

If a 'groups' file is given (with --groups), we will draw squares on the
heatmap. The 'groups' file has the following format, for example:

seq1,seq2 b
seq1 g
seq2 g

This will first draw a square around seq1+seq2 with blue color, then seq1
and seq2 individually with green color.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.mergemat
documentation:
%prog mergemat *.npy

Combine counts from multiple .npy data files.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.get_seqstarts
documentation:
Go through the SQ headers and pull out all sequences with size
greater than the resolution settings, i.e. contains at least a few cells

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.get_distbins
documentation: Get exponentially sized bins for link length

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.bam2mat
documentation:
%prog bam2mat input.bam

Convert bam file to .mat format, which is simply numpy 2D array. Important
parameter is the resolution, which is the cell size. Small cell size lead
to more fine-grained heatmap, but leads to large .mat size and slower
plotting.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.simulate
documentation:
%prog simulate test

Simulate CLM and IDS files with given names.

The simulator assumes several distributions:
- Links are distributed uniformly across genome
- Log10(link_size) are distributed normally
- Genes are distributed uniformly

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.write_last_and_beds
documentation: Write LAST file, query and subject BED files.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.write_clm
documentation: Write CLM file from simulated data.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.density
documentation:
%prog density test.clm

Estimate link density of contigs.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.optimize
documentation:
%prog optimize test.clm

Optimize the contig order and orientation, based on CLM file.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.optimize_ordering
documentation: Optimize the ordering of contigs by Genetic Algorithm (GA).

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.optimize_orientations
documentation: Optimize the orientations of contigs by using heuristic flipping.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.prepare_synteny
documentation: Prepare synteny plots for movie().

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.separate_tour_and_o
documentation:
The tour line typically contains contig list like:
tig00044568+ tig00045748- tig00071055- tig00015093- tig00030900-

This function separates the names from the orientations.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.iter_last_tour
documentation:
Extract last tour from tourfile. The clm instance is also passed in to see
if any contig is covered in the clm.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.iter_tours
documentation:
Extract tours from tourfile. Tourfile contains a set of contig
configurations, generated at each iteration of the genetic algorithm. Each
configuration has two rows, first row contains iteration id and score,
second row contains list of contigs, separated by comma.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.movie
documentation:
%prog movie test.tour test.clm ref.contigs.last

Plot optimization history.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.score
documentation:
%prog score main_results/ cached_data/ contigsfasta

Score the current LACHESIS CLM.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.prepare_ec
documentation: This prepares EC and converts from contig_id to an index.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.score_evaluate
documentation:
SLOW python version of the evaluation function. For benchmarking
purposes only. Do not use in production.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.movieframe
documentation:
%prog movieframe tour test.clm contigs.ref.anchors

Draw heatmap and synteny in the same plot.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.agp
documentation:
%prog agp main_results/ contigs.fasta

Generate AGP file based on LACHESIS output.

variables.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.LB
documentation: Lower bound for golden_array()

variables.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.UB
documentation: Upper bound for golden_array()

variables.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.BB
documentation: Span for golden_array()

classes.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.ContigOrderingLine
documentation: Stores one line in the ContigOrdering file

classes.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.ContigOrdering

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.ContigOrdering.write_agp
documentation: Converts the ContigOrdering file into AGP format

ContigOrdering file as created by LACHESIS, one per chromosome group.
Header contains summary information per group, followed by list of contigs
with given ordering.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.parse_ids
documentation:
IDS file has a list of contigs that need to be ordered. 'recover',
keyword, if available in the third column, is less confident.

tig00015093     46912
tig00035238     46779   recover
tig00030900     119291

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.calculate_densities
documentation:
Calculate the density of inter-contig links per base. Strong contigs
considered to have high level of inter-contig links in the current
partition.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.activate
documentation:
Select contigs in the current partition. This is the setup phase of the
algorithm, and supports two modes:

- "de novo": This is useful at the start of a new run where no tours
  available. We select the strong contigs that have significant number
  of links to other contigs in the partition. We build a histogram of
  link density (# links per bp) and remove the contigs that appear as
  outliers. The orientations are derived from the matrix decomposition
  of the pairwise strandedness matrix O.

- "hotstart": This is useful when there was a past run, with a given
  tourfile. In this case, the active contig list and orientations are
  derived from the last tour in the file.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.evaluate_tour_M
documentation: Use Cythonized version to evaluate the score of a current tour

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.evaluate_tour_P
documentation:
Use Cythonized version to evaluate the score of a current tour,
with better precision on the distance of the contigs.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.evaluate_tour_Q
documentation:
Use Cythonized version to evaluate the score of a current tour,
taking orientation into consideration. This may be the most accurate
evaluation under the right condition.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.flip_all
documentation: Initialize the orientations based on pairwise O matrix.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.flip_whole
documentation: Test flipping all contigs at the same time to see if score improves.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.flip_one
documentation:
Test flipping every single contig sequentially to see if score
improves.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.prune_tour
documentation:
Test deleting each contig and check the delta_score; tour here must
be an array of ints.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.M
documentation:
Contact frequency matrix. Each cell contains how many inter-contig
links between i-th and j-th contigs.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.O
documentation:
Pairwise strandedness matrix. Each cell contains whether i-th and j-th
contig are the same orientation +1, or opposite orientation -1.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.P
documentation:
Contact frequency matrix with better precision on distance between
contigs. In the matrix M, the distance is assumed to be the distance
between mid-points of two contigs. In matrix Q, however, we compute
harmonic mean of the links for the orientation configuration that is
shortest. This offers better precision for the distance between big
contigs.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.hic.CLMFile.Q
documentation:
Contact frequency matrix when contigs are already oriented. This is s a
similar matrix as M, but rather than having the number of links in the
cell, it points to an array that has the actual distances.

modules.name: jcvi.projects.napus
documentation: Scripts for the Brassica napus genome manuscript (Chalhoub et al. Science 2014).
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.projects.napus.cov
documentation:
%prog cov chrA01 chrC01 chr.sizes data AN.CN.1x1.lifted.anchors.simple

Plot coverage graphs between homeologs, the middle panel show the

functions.name: jcvi.projects.napus.fig3

%prog fig3 chrA02,A02,C2,chrC02 chr.sizes all.bed data

Napus Figure 3 displays alignments between quartet chromosomes, inset

functions.name: jcvi.projects.napus.fig4
documentation:
%prog fig4 layout data

Napus Figure 4A displays an example deleted region for quartet chromosomes,

functions.name: jcvi.projects.napus.deletion
documentation:

modules.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps
documentation: Scaffold Ordering with Weighted Maps.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.normalize_lms_axis
documentation: Normalize the axis limits and labels to beautify axis.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.plotratio
documentation:
%prog plotratio JM-2 chr23 JMMale-23

Illustrate physical vs map distance ratio, that were used in the gap estimation algorithm.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.fake
documentation:
%prog fake input.bed

Make fake `scaffolds.fasta`. Use case for this is that sometimes I would
receive just the csv/bed file and I'd like to use path() out of the box.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.compute_score
documentation:
Compute chain score using dynamic programming. If a marker is the same
linkage group as a previous one, we add bonus; otherwise, we penalize the
chain switching.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.split
documentation:
%prog split input.bed

Split suspicious scaffolds. Suspicious scaffolds are those that contain
chunks that map to more than one linkage group. The chunk size can be
modified through --chunk option.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.movie
documentation:
%prog movie input.bed scaffolds.fasta chr1

Visualize history of scaffold OO. The history is contained within the
tourfile, generated by path(). For each historical scaffold OO, the program
plots a separate PDF file. The plots can be combined to show the progression
as a little animation. The third argument limits the plotting to a
specific pseudomolecule, for example `chr1`.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.make_movie
documentation: Make the movie using either ffmpeg or gifsicle.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.estimategaps
documentation:
%prog estimategaps input.bed

Estimate sizes of inter-scaffold gaps. The AGP file generated by path()
command has unknown gap sizes with a generic number of Ns (often 100 Ns).
The AGP file `input.chr.agp` will be modified in-place.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.merge
documentation:
%prog merge map1 map2 map3 ...

Convert csv maps to bed format.

Each input map is csv formatted, for example:

ScaffoldID,ScaffoldPosition,LinkageGroup,GeneticPosition
scaffold_2707,11508,1,0
scaffold_2707,11525,1,1.2
scaffold_759,81336,1,9.7

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.mergebed
documentation:
%prog mergebed map1.bed map2.bed map3.bed ...

Combine bed maps to bed format, adding the map name.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.path
documentation:
%prog path input.bed scaffolds.fasta

Construct golden path given a set of genetic maps. The respective weight for
each map is given in file `weights.txt`. The map with the highest weight is
considered the pivot map. The final output is an AGP file that contains
ordered scaffolds.

Please note that BED file and FASTA file cannot share the same prefix.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.summary
documentation:
%prog summary input.bed scaffolds.fasta

Print out summary statistics per map, followed by consensus summary of
scaffold anchoring based on multiple maps.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.liftover
documentation: Lifts over a bed file from one assembly to another using a chain file.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.build
documentation:
%prog build input.bed scaffolds.fasta

Build associated genome FASTA file and CHAIN file that can be used to lift
old coordinates to new coordinates. The CHAIN file will be used to lift the
original marker positions to new positions in the reconstructed genome. The
new positions of the markers will be reported in *.lifted.bed.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.plot
documentation:
%prog plot input.bed seqid

Plot the matchings between the reconstructed pseudomolecules and the maps.
Two types of visualizations are available in one canvas:

1. Parallel axes, and matching markers are shown in connecting lines;
2. Scatter plot.

functions.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.plotall
documentation:
%prog plotall input.bed

Plot the matchings between the reconstructed pseudomolecules and the maps.
This command will plot each reconstructed object (non-singleton).

classes.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.ScaffoldOO

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.ScaffoldOO.prepare_ec
documentation:
Prepare Evolutionary Computation. This converts scaffold names into
indices (integer) in the scaffolds array.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.ScaffoldOO.assign_order
documentation:
The goal is to assign scaffold orders. To help order the scaffolds, two
dummy node, START and END, mark the ends of the chromosome. We connect
START to each scaffold (directed), and each scaffold to END.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.ScaffoldOO.get_orientation
documentation:
si, sj are two number series. To compute whether these two series have
same orientation or not. We combine them in the two orientation
configurations and compute length of the longest monotonic series.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.ScaffoldOO.fix_tour
documentation: Test each scaffold if dropping does not decrease LMS.

methods.name: jcvi.assembly.allmaps.ScaffoldOO.fix_orientation
documentation:
Test each scaffold if flipping will increass longest monotonic chain
length.

This contains the routine to construct order and orientation for the
scaffolds per partition.

modules.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane
documentation: Simulate sugarcane genomes and analyze the diversity in the progeny genomes.
!/usr/bin/env python3
-*- coding:utf-8 -*-
sugarcane.py
projects
Created by Haibao Tang on 12/02/19
Copyright © 2019 Haibao Tang. All rights reserved.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.plot_summary
documentation:
Plot the distribution of chromosome numbers given simulated samples.

Args:
    ax (Axes): Matplotlib axes.
    samples (list[Genome]): Summarized genomes.

Returns:
    GenomeSummary: Summary statistics of simulated genomes.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.write_chromosomes
documentation:
Write simulated chromosomes to file

Args:
    genomes (list[Genome]): List of simulated genomes.
    filename (str): File path to write to.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.write_SO_percent
documentation:
Write SO % to file

Args:
    summary (GenomeSummary): List of simulated genomes.
    filename (str): File path to write to.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.simulate
documentation:
%prog simulate [2n+n_FDR|2n+n_SDR|nx2+n]

Run simulation on female restitution. There are two modes:
- 2n+n_FDR: merger between a somatic and a germline
- 2n+n_SDR: merger between a recombined germline and a germline (not yet supported)
- nx2+n: merger between a doubled germline and a germline

These two modes would impact the sequence diversity in the progeny
genome in F1, F2, BCn ... the goal of this simulation, is thus to
understand the mode and the spread of such diversity in the hybrid
progenies.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane._get_sizes
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of chromome lengths from a given file.

Args:
    filename ([str]): Path to the input file. Input file is 2-column file
    with rows `seqid length`.
    prefix_length (int): Extract first N characters.
    tag (str): Prepend `tag-` to the seqid.
    target_size (int): Expected genome size. Defaults to None.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.prepare
documentation:
%prog SoChrLen.txt SsChrLen.txt

Calculate lengths from real sugarcane data.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.get_genome_wide_pct
documentation:
Collect genome-wide ungapped percent identity.
Specifically, from file `SS_SR_SO.summary.txt`.

Args:
    summary (str): File that contains per chromosome pct identity info,
    collected via `formats.blast.summary()`.

Returns:
    Dict[tuple, list]: Genome pair to list of pct identities.

functions.name: jcvi.projects.sugarcane.get_anchors_pct
documentation:
Collect CDS-wide ungapped percent identity.

Args:
    filename (str): Input file name, which is a LAST file.

Returns:
    list: List of pct identities from this LAST file.

Plot divergence between and within SS/SR/SO genomes.

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.pasa
documentation: Utilities for submitting PASA jobs and processing PASA results.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.pasa.assemble
documentation:
%prog assemble pasa_db_name genome.fasta transcripts-dn.fasta [transcript-gg.fasta]

Run the PASA alignment assembly pipeline

If two transcript fasta files (Trinity denovo and genome guided) are provided
and the `--compreh` param is enabled, the PASA Comprehensive Transcriptome DB
protocol is followed <http://pasa.sourceforge.net/#A_ComprehensiveTranscriptome>

Using the `--prepare` option creates a shell script with the run commands without
executing the pipeline

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.pasa.compare
documentation:
%prog compare pasa_db_name [--annots_gff3=annotation.gff3]

Run the PASA annotation comparison pipeline

This assumes that PASA alignment assembly has alredy been completed and
run directory contains `genome.fasta` and `transcript.fasta` files.

If `--annots_gff3` is specified, the PASA database is loaded with the annotations
first before starting annotation comparison. Otherwise, it uses previously
loaded annotation data.

Using the `--prepare` option creates a shell script with the run commands without
executing the pipeline

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.pasa.longest
documentation:
%prog longest pasa.fasta output.subclusters.out

Find the longest PASA assembly and label it as full-length. Also removes
transcripts shorter than half the length of the longest, or shorter than
200bp. The assemblies for the same locus is found in
`output.subclusters.out`. In particular the lines that look like:

sub-cluster: asmbl_25 asmbl_26 asmbl_27

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.pasa.consolidate
documentation:
%prog consolidate gffile1 gffile2 ... > consolidated.out

Given 2 or more gff files generated by pasa annotation comparison,
iterate through each locus (shared locus name or overlapping CDS)
and identify same/different isoforms (shared splicing structure)
across the input datasets.

If `slop` is enabled, consolidation will collapse any variation
in terminal UTR lengths, keeping the longest as representative.

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.stats
documentation:
Collect gene statistics based on gff file:
Exon length, Intron length, Gene length, Exon count
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.stats.summary
documentation:
%prog summary gffile fastafile

Print summary stats, including:
- Gene/Exon/Intron
- Number
- Average size (bp)
- Median size (bp)
- Total length (Mb)
- % of genome
- % GC

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.stats.genestats
documentation:
%prog genestats gffile

Print summary stats, including:
- Number of genes
- Number of single-exon genes
- Number of multi-exon genes
- Number of distinct exons
- Number of genes with alternative transcript variants
- Number of predicted transcripts
- Mean number of distinct exons per gene
- Mean number of transcripts per gene
- Mean gene locus size (first to last exon)
- Mean transcript size (UTR, CDS)
- Mean exon size

Stats modeled after barley genome paper Table 1.
A physical, genetic and functional sequence assembly of the barley genome

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.stats.statstable
documentation:
%prog statstable *.gff

Print gene statistics table.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.stats.histogram
documentation:
%prog histogram *.gff

Plot gene statistics based on output of stats. For each gff file, look to
see if the metrics folder (i.e. Exon_Length) contains the data and plot
them.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.stats.stats
documentation:
%prog stats infile.gff

Collect gene statistics based on gff file. There are some terminology issues
here and so normally we call "gene" are actually mRNA, and sometimes "exon"
are actually CDS, but they are configurable.

Thee numbers are written to text file in four separate folders,
corresponding to the four metrics:

Exon length, Intron length, Gene length, Exon count

With data written to disk then you can run %prog histogram

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat
documentation:
Convert common output files from gene prediction software into gff3 format.

Similar to the utilities in DAWGPAWS.
<http://dawgpaws.sourceforge.net/man.html>
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.plot
documentation:
%prog plot tagged.new.bed chr1

Plot gene identifiers along a particular chromosome, often to illustrate the
gene id assignment procedure.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.instantiate
documentation:
%prog instantiate tagged.bed blacklist.ids big_gaps.bed

instantiate NEW genes tagged by renumber.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.prepare
documentation: Remove prepended tags in gene names.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.renumber
documentation:
%prog renumber Mt35.consolidated.bed > tagged.bed

Renumber genes for annotation updates.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.annotate
documentation:
%prog annotate new.bed old.bed 2> log

Annotate the `new.bed` with features from `old.bed` for the purpose of
gene numbering.

Ambiguity in ID assignment can be resolved by either of the following 2 methods:
- `alignment`: make use of global sequence alignment score (calculated by `needle`)
- `overlap`: make use of overlap length (calculated by `intersectBed`)

Transfer over as many identifiers as possible while following guidelines:
http://www.arabidopsis.org/portals/nomenclature/guidelines.jsp#editing

Note: Following RegExp pattern describes the structure of the identifier
assigned to features in the `new.bed` file.

new_id_pat = re.compile(r"^\d+\.[cemtx]+\S+")

Examples: 23231.m312389, 23231.t004898, 23231.tRNA.144
Adjust the value of `new_id_pat` manually as per your ID naming conventions.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.rename
documentation:
%prog rename genes.bed [gaps.bed]

Rename genes for annotation release.

For genes on chromosomes (e.g. the 12th gene on C1):
Bo1g00120

For genes on scaffolds (e.g. the 12th gene on unplaced Scaffold00285):
Bo00285s120

The genes identifiers will increment by 10. So assuming no gap, these are
the consecutive genes:
Bo1g00120, Bo1g00130, Bo1g00140...
Bo00285s120, Bo00285s130, Bo00285s140...

When we encounter gaps, we would like the increment to be larger. For example,
Bo1g00120, <gap>, Bo1g01120...

Gaps bed file is optional.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.parse_prefix
documentation:
Parse identifier such as a|c|le|d|li|re|or|AT4G00480.1 and return
tuple of prefix string (separated at '|') and suffix (AGI identifier)

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.reindex
documentation:
%prog reindex gffile pep.fasta ref.pep.fasta

Reindex the splice isoforms (mRNA) in input GFF file, preferably
generated after PASA annotation update

In the input GFF file, there can be several types of mRNA within a locus:
* CDS matches reference, UTR extended, inherits reference mRNA ID
* CDS (slightly) different from reference, inherits reference mRNA ID
* Novel isoform added by PASA, have IDs like "LOCUS.1.1", "LOCUS.1.2"
* Multiple mRNA collapsed due to shared structure, have IDs like "LOCUS.1-LOCUS.1.1"

In the case of multiple mRNA which have inherited the same reference mRNA ID,
break ties by comparing the new protein with the reference protein using
EMBOSS `needle` to decide which mRNA retains ID and which is assigned a new ID.

All mRNA identifiers should follow the AGI naming conventions.

When reindexing the isoform identifiers, order mRNA based on:
* decreasing transcript length
* decreasing support from multiple input datasets used to run pasa.consolidate()

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.publocus
documentation:
%prog publocus idsfile > idsfiles.publocus

Given a list of model identifiers, convert each into a GenBank approved
pub_locus.

Example output:
Medtr1g007020.1             MTR_1g007020
Medtr1g007030.1             MTR_1g007030
Medtr1g007060.1             MTR_1g007060A
Medtr1g007060.2             MTR_1g007060B

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.augustus
documentation:
%prog augustus augustus.gff3 > reformatted.gff3

AUGUSTUS does generate a gff3 (--gff3=on) but need some refinement.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.tRNAscan
documentation:
%prog tRNAscan all.trna > all.trna.gff3

Convert tRNAscan-SE output into gff3 format.

Sequence                tRNA            Bounds          tRNA    Anti    Intron Bounds   Cove
Name            tRNA #  Begin           End             Type    Codon   Begin   End     Score
--------        ------  ----            ------          ----    -----   -----   ----    ------
23231           1       335355          335440          Tyr     GTA     335392  335404  69.21
23231           2       1076190         1076270         Leu     AAG     0       0       66.33

Conversion based on PERL one-liner in:
<https://github.com/sujaikumar/assemblage/blob/master/README-annotation.md>

classes.name: jcvi.annotation.reformat.Stride
documentation:
Allows four basic strides and three extended strides:
                  __.
      0 10          |
     0 5 10         | basic set of strides
    0 3 7 10        |
   0 2 5 8 10     __|
  0 2 4 6 8 10      |
 0 1 3 5 7 9 10     | extended set of strides
01 23 45 67 89 10 __|

  We have main parameters, # we need, # available go through all possible
  numbers excluding everything in black.

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.evm
documentation:
Wrapper for running series of EVM commands. There are two flavors of running
EVM - TIGR only mode which communicates with the Sybase db; evm mode which
communicates with GFF file.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.evm.maker
documentation:
%prog maker maker.gff3 genome.fasta

Prepare EVM inputs by separating tracks from MAKER.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.evm.tigrload
documentation:
%prog tigrload db ev_type

Load EVM results into TIGR db. Actually, just write a load.sh script. The
ev_type should be set, e.g. "EVM1", "EVM2", etc.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.evm.pasa
documentation:
%prog pasa pasa_db fastafile

Run EVM in TIGR-only mode.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.evm.tigrprepare
documentation:
%prog tigrprepare asmbl.fasta asmbl.ids db pasa.terminal_exons.gff3

Run EVM in TIGR-only mode.

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.trinity
documentation:
Trinity assembly of RNAseq reads. Contains de novo (DN) method and genome-guided
(GG) method.

DN: http://trinityrnaseq.sourceforge.net/
GG: http://trinityrnaseq.sourceforge.net/genome_guided_trinity.html
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.trinity.prepare
documentation:
%prog prepare [--options] folder [--bam rnaseq.coordSorted.bam]

Run Trinity on a folder of reads.  When paired-end (--paired) mode is on,
filenames will be scanned based on whether they contain the patterns
("_1_" and "_2_") or (".1." and ".2.") or ("_1." and "_2.").

By default, prepare script for DN-Trinity.

If coord-sorted BAM is provided, prepare script for GG-Trinity, using BAM
as starting point.

Newer versions of trinity can take multiple fastq files as input.
If "--merge" is specified, the fastq files are merged together before assembling

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.train
documentation: Train ab initio gene predictors.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.train.pasa
documentation:
%prog ${pasadb}.assemblies.fasta ${pasadb}.pasa_assemblies.gff3

Wraps `pasa_asmbls_to_training_set.dbi`.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.train.genemark
documentation:
%prog genemark species fastafile

Train GENEMARK model given fastafile. GENEMARK self-trains so no trainig
model gff file is needed.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.train.snap
documentation:
%prog snap species gffile fastafile

Train SNAP model given gffile and fastafile. Whole procedure taken from:
<http://gmod.org/wiki/MAKER_Tutorial_2012>

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.train.augustus
documentation:
%prog augustus species gffile fastafile

Train AUGUSTUS model given gffile and fastafile. Whole procedure taken from:
<http://www.molecularevolution.org/molevolfiles/exercises/augustus/training.html>

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.maker
documentation:
Utility script for annotations based on MAKER.

Many of the routines in this script is to select among a set of conflicting
models, either through accuracy (batcheval) or simply the length (longest).
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.parallel
documentation:
%prog parallel genome.fasta N

Partition the genome into parts and run separately. This is useful if MAKER
is to be run on the grid.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.merge
documentation:
%prog merge outdir output.gff

Follow-up command after grid jobs are completed after parallel().

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.validate
documentation:
%prog validate outdir genome.fasta

Validate current folder after MAKER run and check for failures. Failed batch
will be written to a directory for additional work.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.batcheval
documentation:
%prog batcheval model.ids gff_file evidences.bed fastafile

Get the accuracy for a list of models against evidences in the range of the
genes. For example:

$ %prog batcheval all.gff3 isoforms.ids proteins.bed scaffolds.fasta

Outfile contains the scores for the models can be found in models.scores

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.get_splits
documentation: Use intersectBed to find the fused gene => split genes mappings.

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.get_accuracy
documentation:
Get sensitivity, specificity and accuracy given gff_file, and a query range
that look like "chr1:1-10000".

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.split
documentation:
%prog split split.bed evidences.bed predictor1.gff predictor2.gff fastafile

Split MAKER models by checking against predictors (such as AUGUSTUS and
FGENESH). For each region covered by a working model. Find out the
combination of predictors that gives the best accuracy against evidences
(such as PASA).

`split.bed` can be generated by pulling out subset from a list of ids
$ python -m jcvi.formats.base join split.ids working.bed
    --column=0,3 --noheader | cut -f2-7 > split.bed

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.maker.datastore
documentation:
%prog datastore datastore.log > gfflist.log

Generate a list of gff filenames to merge. The `datastore.log` file can be
generated by something like:

$ find
/usr/local/scratch/htang/EVM_test/gannotation/maker/1132350111853_default/i1/
-maxdepth 4 -name "*datastore*.log" > datastore.log

modules.name: jcvi.annotation.qc
documentation:
Run quality control (QC) on gene annotation. MAKER output was used during
testing. Several aspects of annotation QC are implemented in this script.

- Trim UTRs. MAKER sometimes predict UTRs that extend into other genes.
- Remove overlapping models.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.qc.uniq
documentation:
%prog uniq gffile cdsfasta

Remove overlapping gene models. Similar to formats.gff.uniq(), overlapping
'piles' are processed, one by one.

Here, we use a different algorithm, that retains the best non-overlapping
subset witin each pile, rather than single best model. Scoring function is
also different, rather than based on score or span, we optimize for the
subset that show the best combined score. Score is defined by:

score = (1 - AED) * length

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.qc.trimUTR
documentation:
%prog trimUTR gffile

Remove UTRs in the annotation set.

If reference GFF3 is provided, reinstate UTRs from reference
transcripts after trimming.

Note: After running trimUTR, it is advised to also run
`python -m jcvi.formats.gff fixboundaries` on the resultant GFF3
to adjust the boundaries of all parent 'gene' features

functions.name: jcvi.annotation.qc.nmd
documentation:
%prog nmd gffile

Identify transcript variants which might be candidates for nonsense
mediated decay (NMD)

A transcript is considered to be a candidate for NMD when the CDS stop
codon is located more than 50nt upstream of terminal splice site donor

References:
http://www.nature.com/horizon/rna/highlights/figures/s2_spec1_f3.html
http://www.biomedcentral.com/1741-7007/7/23/figure/F1

%prog fetch "query"

You can also specify a `filename` which contains queries, one per line.

modules.name: jcvi.apps.gbsubmit
documentation: Prepare the data for Genbank submission
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.apps.gbsubmit.fcs
documentation:
%prog fcs fcsfile

Process the results from Genbank contaminant screen. An example of the file
looks like:

contig name, length, span(s), apparent source
contig0746      11760   1..141  vector
contig0751      14226   13476..14226    vector
contig0800      124133  30512..30559    primer/adapter

functions.name: jcvi.apps.gbsubmit.asn
documentation:
%prog asn asnfiles

Mainly to get this block, and extract `str` field:

    general {
      db "TIGR" ,
      tag
        str "mtg2_12952" } ,
    genbank {
      accession "AC148996" ,

functions.name: jcvi.apps.gbsubmit.htgnew
documentation:
%prog htgnew fastafile phasefile template.sbt

Prepare sqnfiles for submitting new Genbank HTG records.

`fastafile` contains the sequences.
`phasefile` contains the phase information, it is a two column file:

mth2-45h12    3

`template.sbt` is the Genbank submission template.

This function is simpler than htg, since the record names have not be
assigned yet (so less bookkeeping).

functions.name: jcvi.apps.gbsubmit.htg
documentation:
%prog htg fastafile template.sbt

Prepare sqnfiles for Genbank HTG submission to update existing records.

`fastafile` contains the records to update, multiple records are allowed
(with each one generating separate sqn file in the sqn/ folder). The record
defline has the accession ID. For example,
>AC148290.3

Internally, this generates two additional files (phasefile and namesfile)
and download records from Genbank. Below is implementation details:

`phasefile` contains, for each accession, phase information. For example:
AC148290.3      3       HTG     2       mth2-45h12

which means this is a Phase-3 BAC. Record with only a single contig will be
labeled as Phase-3 regardless of the info in the `phasefile`. Template file
is the Genbank sbt template. See jcvi.formats.sbt for generation of such
files.

Another problem is that Genbank requires the name of the sequence to stay
the same when updating and will kick back with a table of name conflicts.
For example:

We are unable to process the updates for these entries
for the following reason:

Seqname has changed

Accession Old seq_name New seq_name
--------- ------------ ------------
AC239792 mtg2_29457 AC239792.1

To prepare a submission, this script downloads genbank and asn.1 format,
and generate the phase file and the names file (use formats.agp.phase() and
apps.gbsubmit.asn(), respectively). These get automatically run.

However, use --phases if the genbank files contain outdated information.
For example, the clone name changes or phase upgrades. In this case, run
formats.agp.phase() manually, modify the phasefile and use --phases to override.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.gbsubmit.convert_96_to_384
documentation:
Convert the 96-well number and quad number to 384-well number

```pycon
>>> convert_96_to_384("B02", 1)
'C3'
>>> convert_96_to_384("H09", 4)
'P18'
```

documentation:
%prog gss fastafile plateMapping

Generate sequence files and metadata templates suited for gss submission.
The FASTA file is assumed to be exported from the JCVI data delivery folder
which looks like:

>1127963806024 /library_name=SIL1T054-B-01-120KB /clear_start=0
/clear_end=839 /primer_id=1049000104196 /trace_id=1064147620169
/trace_file_id=1127963805941 /clone_insert_id=1061064364776
/direction=reverse /sequencer_run_id=1064147620155
/sequencer_plate_barcode=B906423 /sequencer_plate_well_coordinates=C3
/sequencer_plate_96well_quadrant=1 /sequencer_plate_96well_coordinates=B02
/template_plate_barcode=CC0251602AB /growth_plate_barcode=BB0273005AB
AGCTTTAGTTTCAAGGATACCTTCATTGTCATTCCCGGTTATGATGATATCATCAAGATAAACAAGAATG
ACAATGATACCTGTTTGGTTCTGAAGTGTAAAGAGGGTATGTTCAGCTTCAGATCTTCTAAACCCTTTGT
CTAGTAAGCTGGCACTTAGCTTCCTATACCAAACCCTTTGTGATTGCTTCAGTCCATAAATTGCCTTTTT

Plate mapping file maps the JTC `sequencer_plate_barcode` to external IDs.
For example:
B906423 SIL-001

documentation: Procedure to cut genome using restriction enzymes.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

documentation:
%prog digest fastafile NspI,BfuCI

Digest fasta sequences to map restriction site positions.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.restriction.extract_full
documentation: Full extraction of seq flanking the sites.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.restriction.extract_ends
documentation: Extraction of ends of fragments above certain size.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.restriction.fragment
documentation:
%prog fragment fastafile enzyme

Cut the fastafile using the specified enzyme, and grab upstream and
downstream nucleotide sequence along with the cut site. In this case, the
sequences extracted are:

            |- PstI
============|===========
        (-------)

Sometimes we need to limit the size of the restriction fragments, for
example the GBS protocol does not allow fragments larger than 800bp.

       |-PstI        |- PstI              |- PstI
~~~====|=============|==========~~~~~~~===|============
       (---)     (---)

In this case, the second fragment is longer than 800bp, therefore the two
ends are NOT extracted, as in the first fragment.

modules.name: jcvi.apps.grid
documentation: Codes to submit multiple jobs to JCVI grid engine

functions.name: jcvi.apps.grid.array
documentation:
%prog array commands.list

Parallelize a set of commands on grid using array jobs.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.grid.run
documentation:
\%prog run command ::: file1 file2

Parallelize a set of commands on grid. The syntax is modeled after GNU
parallel \<<http://www.gnu.org/s/parallel/man.html#options>>

{} - input line
{.} - input line without extension
{\_} - input line first part
{/} - basename of input line
{/.} - basename of input line without extension
{/\_} - basename of input line first part
{#} - sequence number of job to run
\::: - Use arguments from the command line as input source instead of stdin
(standard input).

If file name is `t/example.tar.gz`, then,
{} is "t/example.tar.gz", {.} is "t/example.tar", {\_} is "t/example"
{/} is "example.tar.gz", {/.} is "example.tar", {/\_} is "example"

A few examples:
ls -1 .fastq | %prog run process {} {.}.pdf # use stdin
\%prog run process {} {.}.pdf ::: fastq # use :::
\%prog run "zcat {} > {.}" ::: .gz # quote redirection
\%prog run < commands.list # run a list of commands

functions.name: jcvi.apps.grid.kill
documentation:
%prog kill [options] JOBNAMEPAT/JOBIDs

Kill jobs based on JOBNAME pattern matching (case-sensitive)
or list of JOBIDs (comma separated)

Examples:
%prog kill "pyth*"                 # Use regex
%prog kill 160253,160245,160252    # Use list of job ids
%prog kill all                     # Everything

classes.name: jcvi.apps.grid.SharedCounter

methods.name: jcvi.apps.grid.SharedCounter.increment
documentation: Increment the counter by n (default = 1)

methods.name: jcvi.apps.grid.SharedCounter.value
documentation: Return the value of the counter

A synchronized shared counter.

The locking done by multiprocessing.Value ensures that only a single
process or thread may read or write the in-memory ctypes object. However,
in order to do n += 1, Python performs a read followed by a write, so a
second process may read the old value before the new one is written by the
first process. The solution is to use a multiprocessing.Lock to guarantee
the atomicity of the modifications to Value.

This class comes almost entirely from Eli Bendersky's blog:
http://eli.thegreenplace.net/2012/01/04/shared-counter-with-pythons-multiprocessing/

classes.name: jcvi.apps.grid.Queue

methods.name: jcvi.apps.grid.Queue.qsize
documentation: Reliable implementation of multiprocessing.Queue.qsize()

methods.name: jcvi.apps.grid.Queue.empty
documentation: Reliable implementation of multiprocessing.Queue.empty()

A portable implementation of multiprocessing.Queue.

Because of multithreading / multiprocessing semantics, Queue.qsize() may
raise the NotImplementedError exception on Unix platforms like Mac OS X
where sem_getvalue() is not implemented. This subclass addresses this
problem by using a synchronized shared counter (initialized to zero) and
increasing / decreasing its value every time the put() and get() methods
are called, respectively. This not only prevents NotImplementedError from
being raised, but also allows us to implement a reliable version of both
qsize() and empty().

modules.name: jcvi.apps.align
documentation:
Perform DNA-DNA alignment using BLAST, NUCMER and BLAT. Keep the interface the
same and does parallelization both in core and on grid.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.run_vecscreen
documentation:
BLASTN parameters reference:
http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/VecScreen/VecScreen_docs.html

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.minimap
documentation:
%prog minimap ref.fasta query.fasta

Wrap minimap2 aligner using query against sequences. When query and ref
is the same, we are in "self-scan" mode (e.g. useful for finding internal
duplications resulted from mis-assemblies).

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.nucmer
documentation:
%prog nucmer ref.fasta query.fasta

Run NUCMER using query against reference. Parallel implementation derived
from: <https://github.com/fritzsedlazeck/sge_mummer>

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.blasr
documentation:
%prog blasr ref.fasta fofn

Run blasr on a set of PacBio reads. This is based on a divide-and-conquer
strategy described below.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.blat
documentation:
%prog blat ref.fasta query.fasta

Calls blat and filters BLAST hits.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.blast
documentation:
%prog blast ref.fasta query.fasta

Calls blast and then filter the BLAST hits. Default is megablast.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.lastgenome
documentation:
%prog genome_A.fasta genome_B.fasta

Run LAST by calling LASTDB, LASTAL. The script runs the following steps:
$ lastdb -P0 -uNEAR -R01 Chr10A-NEAR Chr10A.fa
$ lastal -E0.05 -C2 Chr10A-NEAR Chr10A.fa -fTAB > Chr10A.Chr10A.tab
$ last-dotplot Chr10A.Chr10A.tab

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.lastgenomeuniq
documentation:
%prog genome_A.fasta genome_B.fasta

Run LAST by calling LASTDB, LASTAL and LAST-SPLIT. The recipe is based on
tutorial here:

<https://github.com/mcfrith/last-genome-alignments>

The script runs the following steps:
$ lastdb -P0 -uNEAR -R01 Chr10A-NEAR Chr10A.fa
$ lastal -E0.05 -C2 Chr10A-NEAR Chr10B.fa | last-split -m1 | maf-swap | last-split -m1 -fMAF > Chr10A.Chr10B.1-1.maf
$ maf-convert -n blasttab Chr10A.Chr10B.1-1.maf > Chr10A.Chr10B.1-1.blast

Works with LAST v959.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.last
documentation:
%prog database.fasta query.fasta

Run LAST by calling LASTDB and LASTAL. LAST program available:
<http://last.cbrc.jp>

Works with LAST-719.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.blast_main
documentation:
%prog database.fasta query.fasta

Run blastp/blastn by calling BLAST+ blastp/blastn depends on dbtype.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.align.diamond_blastp_main
documentation:
%prog database.fasta query.fasta

modules.name: jcvi.apps.phylo
documentation:
Construct and visualize phylogenetic trees from:
1.  MCSCAN output
2.  CDS sequences in FASTA format

Options are provided for each step:
1.  sequence alignment:
    ClustalW2 or MUSCLE (wrapped on Biopython)
2.  alignment editting:
    GBlocks (optional)
3.  build trees:
    NJ: PHYLIP
    ML: RAxML or PHYML

Optional steps:
- reroot tree
- alternative topology test (SH test)
- TreeFix

The external software needs be installed first.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.run_treefix
documentation: get the ML tree closest to the species tree

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.run_gblocks
documentation: remove poorly aligned positions and divergent regions with Gblocks

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.run_ffitch
documentation: Infer tree branch lengths using ffitch in EMBOSS PHYLIP

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.smart_reroot
documentation:
simple function to reroot Newick format tree using ete2

Tree reading format options see here:
http://packages.python.org/ete2/tutorial/tutorial_trees.html#reading-newick-trees

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.build_nj_phylip
documentation:
build neighbor joining tree of DNA seqs with PHYLIP in EMBOSS

PHYLIP manual
http://evolution.genetics.washington.edu/phylip/doc/

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.build_ml_phyml
documentation: build maximum likelihood tree of DNA seqs with PhyML

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.build_ml_raxml
documentation: build maximum likelihood tree of DNA seqs with RAxML

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.SH_raxml
documentation:
SH test using RAxML

querytree can be a single tree or a bunch of trees (eg. from bootstrapping)

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.subalignment
documentation:
Subset synonymous or fourfold degenerate sites from an alignment

input should be a codon alignment

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.merge_rows_local
documentation: merge overlapping rows within given row count distance

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.add_tandems
documentation: add tandem genes to anchor genes in mcscan file

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.prepare
documentation:
%prog prepare mcscanfile cdsfile [options]

Pick sequences from cdsfile to form fasta files, according to multiple
alignment in the mcscanfile.
The fasta sequences can then be used to construct phylogenetic tree.

Use --addtandem=tandemfile to collapse tandems of anchors into single row.
The tandemfile must be provided with *ALL* genomes involved, otherwise
result will be incomplete and redundant.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.build
documentation:
%prog build [prot.fasta] cds.fasta [options] --outdir=outdir

This function wraps on the following steps:
1. msa using ClustalW2 or MUSCLE(default)
2. (optional) alignment editing using Gblocks
3. build NJ tree using PHYLIP in EMBOSS package
   seq names should be unique by first 10 chars (restriction of PHYLIP)
4. build ML tree using RAxML(default) or PHYML, use keywords raxml or phyml,
   *WARNING* maybe slow with large dataset

If an outgroup file is provided, the result tree will be rooted on the
outgroup according to order in the file, i.e. the name in row1 will be
tried first. If not found, row2 will be used, etc.
Tail truncated names can be provided so long as it is unique among the seqs.
If not uniq, the first occurrence will be used. For example, if you have
two moss sequences in your input, then the tree will be rooted on the
first moss sequence encountered by the program, unless they are monophylic,
 in which case the root will be their common ancestor.

--stree and --smap are required if --treefix is set.

Trees can be edited again using an editor such as Dendroscope. This
is the recommended way to get highly customized trees.

Newick format trees will be deposited into outdir (. by default).

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo._draw_trees
documentation: Draw one or multiple trees on one plot.

functions.name: jcvi.apps.phylo.draw
documentation:
%prog draw --input newicktrees [options]

Draw phylogenetic trees into single or combined plots.
Input trees should be one of the following:
1.  single Newick format tree file
2.  a dir containing *ONLY* the tree files to be drawn

Newick format:
http://evolution.genetics.washington.edu/phylip/newicktree.html

This function wraps on jcvi.graphics.tree
This function is better used for trees generated by jcvi.apps.phylo (rooted

modules.name: jcvi.compara.quota
documentation:
Quota synteny alignment (QUOTA-ALIGN)

%prog [options] anchorsfile --qbed=qbedfile --sbed=sbedfile

This python program does the following:
1. merge 2D-overlapping blocks (now skipped, but existed in original version)
2. build constraints that represent 1D-overlap among blocks
3. feed the data into the linear programming solver

The algorithm is described in Tang et al. BMC Bioinformatics 2011.
"Screening synteny blocks in pairwise genome comparisons through integer
programming."
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

modules.name: jcvi.compara.blastfilter
documentation:
\%prog blast_file --qbed query.bed --sbed subject.bed

Accepts bed format and blast file, and run several BLAST filters below:

```
* Local dup filter:
```

if the input is query.bed and subject.bed, the script files query.localdups
and subject.localdups are created containing the parent|offspring dups, as
inferred by subjects hitting same query or queries hitting same subject.

- C-score filter:

see supplementary info for sea anemone genome paper, formula:

```
cscore(A,B) = score(A,B) /
     max(best score for A, best score for B)
```

Finally a blast.filtered file is created.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

$ %prog less myfile 0.1      # Go to 10% of the current file and streaming

functions.name: jcvi.apps.base.sample_N
documentation:
When size of N is > size of a, random.sample() will emit an error:
ValueError: sample larger than population

This method handles such restrictions by repeatedly sampling when that
happens. Guaranteed to cover all items if N is > size of a.

Examples:
\>>> sample_N([1, 2, 3], 2, seed=666)
[2, 3]
\>>> sample_N([1, 2, 3], 3, seed=666)
[2, 3, 1]
\>>> sample_N([1, 2, 3], 4, seed=666)
[2, 3, 1, 2]

modules.name: jcvi.compara.pad
documentation:
This implements the method described in Tang et al. 2010 PNAS paper,
<http://www.pnas.org/content/107/1/472>

Angiosperm genome comparisons reveal early polyploidy in the monocot lineage

The main pipeline assumes starting with defined synteny blocks in .anchors
format (use compara.synteny.scan()), then segment the chromosomes and cluster
segments according to the matching patterns. Finally the putative ancestral
regions (PAR) are identified and visualized.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.compara.pad.make_arrays
documentation:
This function makes three matrices: observed, expected and logmp. The logmp
contains the statistical significance for each comparison.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.pad.pad
documentation:
%prog pad blastfile cdtfile --qbed q.pad.bed --sbed s.pad.bed

Test and reconstruct candidate PADs.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.pad.get_segments
documentation:
Given a list of Range, perform chaining on the ranges and select a highest
scoring subset and cut based on their boundaries. Let's say the projection
of the synteny blocks onto one axis look like the following.

1=====10......20====30....35====~~

Then the segmentation will yield a block [1, 20), [20, 35), using an
arbitrary right extension rule. Extra are additional end breaks for
chromosomes.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.pad.cluster
documentation:
%prog cluster blastfile anchorfile --qbed qbedfile --sbed sbedfile

Cluster the segments and form PAD. This is the method described in Tang et
al. (2010) PNAS paper. The anchorfile defines a list of synteny blocks,
based on which the genome on one or both axis can be chopped up into pieces
and clustered.

modules.name: jcvi.compara.reconstruct
documentation:
From synteny blocks, reconstruct ancestral order by interleaving the genes in
between the anchors. This is the bottom-up method used first in Bowers (2003),
and in Tang (2010), to reconstruct pre-alpha and pre-rho order, respectively.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

genome 0

chr 1
138 6133 -5387 144 -6132 -139 140 141 146 -147 6134 145 -170 -142 -143

methods.name: jcvi.compara.pedigree.GenotypeCollection.inbreeding_coef
documentation:
Calculate inbreeding coefficient for a sample.

Traditional inbreeding coefficient (F) is a measure of the probability
that two alleles at a locus are identical by descent. This definition is
not applicable for polyploids.

Here we use a simpler measure of inbreeding coefficient, which is the
proportion of alleles that are non-unique in a genotype. Or we should
really call it "Proportion inbred".

modules.name: jcvi.compara.ks
documentation: Calculation of synonymous substitutions (Ks).
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.batch
documentation:
%prog batch all.cds *.anchors

Compute Ks values for a set of anchors file. This will generate a bunch of
work directories for each comparisons. The anchorsfile should be in the form
of specie1.species2.anchors.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.multireport
documentation:
%prog multireport layoutfile

Generate several Ks value distributions in the same figure. If the layout
file is missing then a template file listing all ks files will be written.

The layout file contains the Ks file, number of components, colors, and labels:

# Ks file, ncomponents, label, color, marker
LAP.sorghum.ks, 1, LAP-sorghum, r, o
SES.sorghum.ks, 1, SES-sorghum, g, +
MOL.sorghum.ks, 1, MOL-sorghum, m, ^

If color or marker is missing, then a random one will be assigned.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.gc3
documentation:
%prog gc3 ksfile cdsfile [cdsfile2] -o newksfile

Filter the Ks results to remove high GC3 genes. High GC3 genes are
problematic in Ks calculation - see Tang et al. 2010 PNAS. Specifically, the
two calculation methods produce drastically different results for these
pairs. Therefore we advise to remoeve these high GC3 genes. This is often
the case for studying cereal genes.

If 2 genomes are involved, the cdsfile of the 2nd genome can be provided
concatenated or separated.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.extract_pairs
documentation: Called by fromgroups(), extract pairs specific to a pair of species.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.fromgroups
documentation:
%prog fromgroups groupsfile a.bed b.bed ...

Flatten the gene familes into pairs, the groupsfile is a file with each line
containing the members, separated by comma. The commands also require
several bed files in order to sort the pairs into different piles (e.g.
pairs of species in comparison.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.prepare
documentation:
%prog prepare pairsfile cdsfile [pepfile] -o paired.cds.fasta

Pick sequences from cdsfile to form pairs, ready to be calculated. The
pairsfile can be generated from formats.blast.cscore(). The first two
columns contain the pair.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.calc
documentation:
%prog calc [prot.fasta] cds.fasta > out.ks

Protein file is optional. If only one file is given, it is assumed to
be CDS sequences with correct frame (frame 0). Results will be written to
stdout. Both protein file and nucleotide file are assumed to be Fasta format,
with adjacent records as the pairs to compare.

Author: Haibao Tang <bao@uga.edu>, Brad Chapman, Jingping Li
Calculate synonymous mutation rates for gene pairs

This does the following:
    1. Fetches a protein pair.
    2. Aligns the protein pair with clustalw (default) or muscle.
    3. Convert the output to Fasta format.
    4. Use this alignment info to align gene sequences using PAL2NAL
    5. Run PAML yn00 to calculate synonymous mutation rates.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.find_synonymous
documentation: Run yn00 to find the synonymous subsitution rate for the alignment.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.ks.extract_subs_value
documentation:
Extract a subsitution value from a line of text.

    This is just a friendly function to grab a float value for Ks and Kn
    values from the junk I get from the last line of the yn00 file.

    Line:
    2    1    52.7   193.3   2.0452  0.8979  0.0193 0.0573 +- 0.0177
    2.9732 +- 3.2002

    Parts:
        ['   2    1    52.7   193.3   2.0452  0.8979  0.0193 0.0573',
         ' 0.0177  2.9732', ' 3.2002
']

    So we want 0.0573 for Kn and 2.9732 for Ks.

modules.name: jcvi.compara.__main__
documentation: Cluster of comparative genomics analysis methods: SynFind and QUOTA-ALIGN algorithms, synteny analysis, QC, etc.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

modules.name: jcvi.compara.synfind
documentation:
%prog rice.sorghum.last --qbed=rice.bed --sbed=sorghum.bed

Given a blast, we find the syntenic regions for every single gene. The
algorithm works by expanding the query gene to a window centered on the gene. A
single linkage algorithm follows that outputs the synteny block.

The result looks like the following:
Os01g0698300    Sb03g032090     S    7     +
Os01g0698500    Sb03g032140     G    11    +

The pairs (A, B) -- A is query, and then B is the syntenic region found.
G is "Gray gene", which means it does not have match to the region (fractionated
or inserted). In this case, a right flanker is used to represent the region.
S is "Syntelog", which means it has a match to the region. In this case, the match
itself is used to represent the region.  The number in the 4th column is the
synteny score. For the same query, it is ordered with decreasing synteny score.
The last column means orientation. "+" is same direction.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.compara.synfind.find_synteny_region
documentation:
Get all synteny blocks for a query, algorithm is single linkage
anchors are a window centered on query

Two categories of syntenic regions depending on what query is:
(Syntelog): syntenic region is denoted by the syntelog
(Gray gene): syntenic region is marked by the closest flanker

modules.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation
documentation: Catalog gene losses, and bites within genes.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.offdiag
documentation:
%prog offdiag diploid.napus.1x1.lifted.anchors

Find gene pairs that are off diagnoal. "Off diagonal" are the pairs that are
not on the orthologous chromosomes. For example, napus chrA01 and brapa A01.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.diff
documentation:
%prog diff simplefile

Calculate difference of pairwise syntenic regions.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.estimate_size
documentation:
Estimate the bp length for the deletion tracks, indicated by the gene accns.
True different levels of estimates vary on conservativeness.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.segment
documentation:
%prog segment loss.ids bedfile

Merge adjacent gene loss into segmental loss.

Then based on the segmental loss, estimate amount of DNA loss in base pairs.
Two estimates can be given:
- conservative: just within the start and end of a single gene
- aggressive: extend the deletion track to the next gene

The real deletion size is within these estimates.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.merge
documentation:
%prog merge protein-quartets registry LOST

Merge protein quartets table with dna quartets registry. This is specific
to the napus project.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.gffselect
documentation:
%prog gffselect gmaplocation.bed expectedlocation.bed translated.ids tag

Try to match up the expected location and gmap locations for particular
genes. translated.ids was generated by fasta.translate --ids. tag must be
one of "complete|pseudogene|partial".

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.gaps
documentation:
%prog gaps idsfile fractionationfile gapsbed

Check gene locations against gaps. `idsfile` contains a list of IDs to query
into `fractionationfile` in order to get expected locations.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.genestatus
documentation:
%prog genestatus diploid.gff3.exon.ids

Tag genes based on translation from GMAP models, using fasta.translate()
--ids.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.summary
documentation:
%prog summary diploid.napus.fractionation gmap.status

Provide summary of fractionation. `fractionation` file is generated with
loss(). `gmap.status` is generated with genestatus().

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.napus
documentation:
%prog napus napus.bed brapa.boleracea.i1.blocks diploid.napus.fractionation

Extract napus gene loss vs diploid ancestors. We are looking specifically
for anything that has the pattern:

    BR - BO    or     BR - BO
    |                       |
    AN                     CN

Step 1: extract BR - BO syntenic pairs
Step 2: get diploid gene retention patterns from BR or BO as query
Step 3: look for if AN or CN is NS(non-syntenic) or NF(not found) and
specifically with NS, the NS location is actually the homeologous site.
Step 4: categorize gene losses into singleton, or segmental (defined as
consecutive losses with a maximum skip of 1

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.loss
documentation:
%prog loss a.b.i1.blocks [a.b-genomic.blast]

Extract likely gene loss candidates between genome a and b.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.fractionation.validate
documentation:
%prog validate diploid.napus.fractionation cds.bed

Check whether [S] intervals overlap with CDS.

modules.name: jcvi.graphics.coverage
documentation:
%prog chrC01 chr.sizes data

Read coverage histogram, similar to wiggle plot. Data contains all the track
data in the form of tab-delimited (x, y) lists.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

modules.name: jcvi.graphics.dotplot
documentation:
%prog [anchorfile|ksfile] --qbed query.bed --sbed subject.bed

visualize the anchorfile in a dotplot. anchorfile contains two columns
indicating gene pairs, followed by an optional column (e.g. Ks value).

The option --colormap specifies the block color to highlight certain blocks in
a file.  Block ids are 1-based (non-digit chars will be removed). For example, below
requests that block 1 is class 'sigma' and block 2 is class 'tau'.

1    sigma
2    tau
3    tau

These classes will be mapped to auto-assigned colors and figure legend added to
the bottom of the figure.

*Important*

Before running this script it is recommended to check/install
TeX Live (http://www.tug.org/texlive/) and
Ghostscript (http://www.ghostscript.com/)
see more here: http://matplotlib.sourceforge.net/users/usetex.html
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.dotplot.downsample
documentation: Downsample the data to a manageable size for plotting.

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.dotplot.dotplot
documentation: Draw a dotplot from an anchor file.

methods.name: jcvi.graphics.dotplot.Palette.__init__
documentation:
Instantiate a palette to map from block_id to color

Args:
    palettedict (Dict, optional): Get the mapping from a dict. Defaults to None.
    palettefile (str, optional): Get the mapping from a two-column file. Defaults to None.

methods.name: jcvi.graphics.dotplot.Palette.from_block_orientation
documentation:
Generate a palette which contains mapping from block_id (1-based) to colors.

Args:
    anchorfile (str): Path to the .anchors file
    qbed (BedFile): Query BED
    sbed (BedFile): Subject BED
    forward_color (str, optional): Color of forward block. Defaults to "#e7298a".
    reverse_color (str, optional): Color of reverse block. Defaults to "#3690c0".

modules.name: jcvi.compara.catalog
documentation: !/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.enrich
documentation:
%prog enrich omgfile groups ntaxa > enriched.omg

Enrich OMG output by pulling genes misses by OMG.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.sort_layout
documentation:
Sort the syntelog table according to chromomomal positions. First orient the
contents against threadbed, then for contents not in threadbed, insert to
the nearest neighbor.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.layout
documentation:
%prog layout omgfile taxa

Build column formatted gene lists after omgparse(). Use species list
separated by comma in place of taxa, e.g. "BR,BO,AN,CN"

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.omgparse
documentation:
%prog omgparse work

Parse the OMG outputs to get gene lists.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.group
documentation:
%prog group anchorfiles

Group the anchors into ortho-groups. Can input multiple anchor files.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.omg
documentation:
%prog omg weightsfile

Run Sankoff's OMG algorithm to get orthologs. Download OMG code at:
<http://137.122.149.195/IsbraSoftware/OMGMec.html>

This script only writes the partitions, but not launch OMGMec. You may need to:

$ parallel "java -cp ~/code/OMGMec TestOMGMec {} 4 > {}.out" ::: work/gf?????

Then followed by omgparse() to get the gene lists.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.omgprepare
documentation:
%prog omgprepare ploidy anchorsfile blastfile

Prepare to run Sankoff's OMG algorithm to get orthologs.

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.ortholog
documentation:
%prog ortholog species_a species_b

Run a sensitive pipeline to find orthologs between two species a and b.
The pipeline runs LAST and generate .lifted.anchors.

`--full` mode would assume 1-to-1 quota synteny blocks as the backbone of
such predictions. Extra orthologs will be recruited from reciprocal best
match (RBH).

functions.name: jcvi.compara.catalog.tandem
documentation:
%prog tandem blast_file cds_file bed_file [options]

Find tandem gene clusters that are separated by N genes, based on filtered
blast_file by enforcing alignments between any two genes at least 50%
(or user specified value) of either gene.

pep_file can also be used in same manner.

modules.name: jcvi.graphics.assembly
documentation:
Assembly QC plots, including general statistics, base and mate coverages, and
scaffolding consistencies.
!/usr/bin/env python
-*- coding: UTF-8 -*-

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.assembly.covlen
documentation:
%prog covlen covfile fastafile

Plot coverage vs length. `covfile` is two-column listing contig id and
depth of coverage.

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.assembly.coverage
documentation:
%prog coverage fastafile ctg bedfile1 bedfile2 ..

Plot coverage from a set of BED files that contain the read mappings. The
paired read span will be converted to a new bedfile that contain the happy
mates. ctg is the chr/scf/ctg that you want to plot the histogram on.

If the bedfiles already contain the clone spans, turn on --spans.

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.assembly.scaffold
documentation:
%prog scaffold scaffold.fasta synteny.blast synteny.sizes synteny.bed
                     physicalmap.blast physicalmap.sizes physicalmap.bed

As evaluation of scaffolding, visualize external line of evidences:
* Plot synteny to an external genome
* Plot alignments to physical map
* Plot alignments to genetic map (TODO)

Each trio defines one panel to be plotted. blastfile defines the matchings
between the evidences vs scaffolds. Then the evidence sizes, and evidence
bed to plot dot plots.

This script will plot a dot in the dot plot in the corresponding location
the plots are one contig/scaffold per plot.

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.assembly.qc
documentation:
%prog qc prefix

Expects data files including:
1. `prefix.bedpe` draws Bezier curve between paired reads
2. `prefix.sizes` draws length of the contig/scaffold
3. `prefix.gaps.bed` mark the position of the gaps in sequence
4. `prefix.bed.coverage` plots the base coverage
5. `prefix.pairs.bed.coverage` plots the clone coverage

See assembly.coverage.posmap() for the generation of these files.

functions.name: jcvi.graphics.assembly.A50
documentation:
%prog A50 contigs_A.fasta contigs_B.fasta ...

Plots A50 graphics, see blog post (http://blog.malde.org/index.php/a50/)
